summary: DHH applauds Netflix's updated cultural guidelines that tell employees they must support the company's content breadth or leave, framed as a response to the failed employee campaign to cancel Dave Chappelle's comedy special. He argues this kind of boundary-setting used to be uncontroversial—workers at publishing houses didn't need to personally approve every book. DHH traces the problem to tech companies blurring work-life boundaries by calling themselves 'families' and demanding total devotion, which collapsed employees' personal identities into their employer's identity. He sees Netflix's move as a healthy reset of expectations.
key_points: Netflix codified that employees must support content breadth even if it conflicts with personal values, creating clear employment boundaries
The expectation that workers don't personally endorse everything their employer produces used to be uncontroversial and widely understood
Tech companies eroded work-life separation by demanding passion and calling themselves families, making employees feel morally liable for all company decisions
Overreach by activist employees invites institutional counter-responses that formalize boundaries more explicitly than before
money_quotes: That's what happens after a moment of overreach. It leaves you exposed for the riposte.

By codifying the fact that Netflix expects employees to be able to work on a wide breadth of content, which invariably means some of it might offend individuals from time to time, they've drawn the boundaries of employment.
Being, say, a proof reader at a publishing house did not mean you had to personally approve of every book that landed on your desk.

In our brave new world, it's become common place to consider your job an integral part of your identity. And thus feeling like you're personally and morally liable for all decisions made and actions taken by said company.

After a few decades of tech companies insisting they're really families, craving unbridled passion from employees, and demanding a work-life balance that was all work and no life, the space and time needed for a personal, private identity has effectively been under corporate occupation.
It's an admission in part that the strategy of boundless accommodation isn't working well for anyone.
key_insight: When companies blur the line between work and identity by demanding total devotion, they shouldn't be surprised when employees treat every company decision as a personal moral matter—and resetting those boundaries is overdue.
